summary: Gruber methodically argues that a rumored 7.85-inch iPad Mini is both technically feasible and strategically necessary for Apple. He walks through the math on display size, tap targets, and Fitts's Law to show the device could work at the interface level. He addresses Steve Jobs's famous 2010 dismissal of 7-inch tablets by noting the size difference, Jobs's pattern of reversals, and that Jobs was really attacking specific bad products, not the concept itself. The piece concludes that Apple may need to sacrifice its typical margins to price the device at $199, preventing Google and Amazon from establishing a price umbrella in the tablet market. Gruber frames this as a tension between short-term profit margins and long-term platform dominance, invoking Jobs's own warning about Apple choosing profits over market share in the late 1980s.
key_points: A 7.85-inch iPad would have tap targets exactly the same physical size as the iPhone, and Fitts's Law suggests smaller screens can use smaller targets because fingers travel less distance

Jobs's 2010 attack on 7-inch tablets was really about specific bad products (the $600 Galaxy Tab running phone Android), not the form factor itself — and Jobs had a long pattern of publicly dismissing ideas Apple later embraced
Apple has never introduced a new iOS form factor with a retina display — non-retina at launch is the pattern, not the exception
The strategic case for aggressive pricing comes from Jobs's own analysis of Apple's 1980s mistake: choosing profits over market share at a critical juncture
Tim Cook's 'price umbrella' philosophy suggests Apple won't let competitors establish a foothold in cheaper tablets unchallenged
money_quotes: If you take an iPad and hold it upright in portrait view, and draw an imaginary horizontal line halfway down the screen, the screens on these seven-inch tablets are a bit smaller than the bottom half of the iPad's display.
A 44-point tap target on the rumored iPad Mini would be exactly the same physical size as a 44-point tap target on the iPhone.
All that matters is whether a smaller iPad would actually be good. If Apple ships it, it will be judged on its own merits, regardless of what Steve Jobs said 18 months ago.
At the critical juncture in the late '80s, when they should have gone for market share, they went for profits. They made obscene profits for several years. And their products became mediocre.
The tension is between short-term profit margins and long-term profit from maximizing market share as soon as possible, while the post-PC market landscape remains nascent and fluid.

A price umbrella is a term used to describe the effect a dominant company can have on a particular market with a popular-yet-expensive product: competitors can enter the market with other products at lower prices and gain customers just based on affordability.
Although if they did, I'll bet it would be the nicest sandpaper in the world.
key_insight: The iPad Mini isn't about whether Apple can make a good smaller tablet — the math on tap targets and Fitts's Law suggests it can — but whether Apple will sacrifice short-term margins to prevent competitors from establishing a price umbrella in the nascent tablet market.
